content.text: Chunking is the step that turns a normalised text document into a list of passages small enough to embed meaningfully and large enough to carry a coherent idea. The choice of chunking strategy has a larger impact on retrieval quality than almost any other indexing decision — yet many pipelines use the default fixed-size splitter without ever questioning it.

attrs.alt: Four chunking strategies: Fixed-size (uniform token windows with overlap), Recursive (splits at paragraph → sentence → word boundaries), Semantic (splits where sentence-to-sentence cosine similarity drops), Hierarchical (small child chunks for ANN, large parent chunks returned to LLM). Bottom: strategy selection guide by retrieval precision, context quality, indexing cost.

content.text: (small chunks match queries more exactly) and
(large chunks give the LLM more surrounding information). Every strategy strikes a different balance.
Split the text into windows of exactly
tokens, with an optional overlap of
tokens carried from the end of the previous chunk into the start of the next. Simple, fast, and predictable — but blind to sentence and paragraph structure.

from langchain_text_splitters import CharacterTextSplitter

# Character-based fixed split (fast, rough)
splitter = CharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,       # target character count
    chunk_overlap=64,     # characters shared between consecutive chunks
    separator="
",       # try to split at newlines first
)
chunks = splitter.split_text(text)
print(f"{len(chunks)} chunks, sizes: {[len(c) for c in chunks[:5]]}")

uniform-density text (chat logs, CSV rows, code files) where every position is equally likely to be relevant. For prose, fixed-size splitting frequently cuts mid-sentence, which hurts embedding quality.
The most widely-used strategy. It tries a list of separators in priority order — paragraph break (
), space — falling back to the next separator only if the chunk would still exceed
. This respects natural document structure as closely as possible while still enforcing a size bound.

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=64,
    separators=["

", "
", ". ", " ", ""],   # priority order
    length_function=len,   # swap for tiktoken for exact token control
)

chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)
print(f"{len(docs)} docs → {len(chunks)} chunks")
Character count overestimates chunk size for languages with multi-byte characters and underestimates for contractions. Use tiktoken to count the actual tokens the embedding model will see:

import tiktoken
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")   # same tokeniser as text-embedding-3-small

def token_length(text: str) -> int:
    return len(enc.encode(text))

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,         # tokens
    chunk_overlap=64,       # tokens
    length_function=token_length,
    separators=["

", "
", ". ", " ", ""],
)
chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)
# Every chunk is guaranteed ≤ 512 tokens
with chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64, and tiktoken-based length_function. This is the right starting point for 80 % of RAG use cases.

Embed each sentence individually, then compare the cosine similarity between consecutive sentences. A sharp similarity drop signals a topic change — that is where the splitter inserts a chunk boundary. The result keeps coherent topics together even if they span variable numbers of sentences.
is determined by the chosen percentile of the similarity distribution.

# pip install langchain-experimental
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

chunker = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings,
    breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",   # split at drops below Nth percentile
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=95,           # 95th percentile = only the sharpest drops
    # Alternatives:
    # breakpoint_threshold_type="standard_deviation"   # split beyond 1 SD below mean
    # breakpoint_threshold_type="interquartile"        # IQR-based
)

chunks = chunker.split_documents(docs)
print(f"Produced {len(chunks)} semantic chunks")
print("Sizes:", [len(c.page_content) for c in chunks[:5]])

semantic chunking requires embedding every sentence during indexing — roughly 5–20× more embedding calls than a recursive splitter on the same text. For a corpus of 10 000 documents this is rarely prohibitive, but for daily incremental indexing the extra cost is a factor. Use it when document structure is inconsistent or topics shift within paragraphs.
in the vector store (for precise ANN matching) but retrieve and return the full
to the LLM (for rich context). The child-to-parent link is stored in a separate document store. LangChain implements this pattern as

from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever
from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

# Parent splitter: large chunks (sent to LLM)
parent_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=2000, chunk_overlap=200)

# Child splitter: small chunks (stored in vector store for ANN)
child_splitter  = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=128,  chunk_overlap=20)

# Stores
vectorstore = Chroma(embedding_function=OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"))
docstore    = InMemoryStore()   # maps parent_id → parent Document

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=docstore,
    child_splitter=child_splitter,
    parent_splitter=parent_splitter,
)

# Index: splits into parents, then children; stores parent IDs in child metadata
retriever.add_documents(docs)

# Retrieve: ANN matches child → look up parent → return parent text to LLM
results = retriever.invoke("What drove Q3 2025 revenue growth?")
print(len(results[0].page_content))   # ~2000 chars — full parent section

long structured documents (technical manuals, legal contracts, annual reports) where a specific fact may appear in a short sentence but the LLM needs the surrounding section to interpret it correctly.
A lighter alternative to hierarchical chunking. Index individual sentences for precise ANN retrieval. At retrieval time, expand each matching sentence by
surrounding sentences (the "window") before passing to the LLM. No separate document store is needed — the expansion is done in memory.

import re
from langchain_core.documents import Document

def sentence_window_split(
    text: str,
    metadata: dict,
    window: int = 2,           # sentences before and after
) -> list[Document]:
    # Split into sentences with a simple regex
    sentences = re.split(r"(?<=[.!?])\s+", text.strip())
    docs = []
    for idx, sent in enumerate(sentences):
        # Context window: ±window sentences around the indexed sentence
        lo = max(0, idx - window)
        hi = min(len(sentences), idx + window + 1)
        context = " ".join(sentences[lo:hi])
        docs.append(Document(
            page_content=sent,       # indexed (ANN uses this vector)
            metadata={
                **metadata,
                "window_context": context,   # returned to LLM
                "sentence_index": idx,
            }
        ))
    return docs

# At retrieval time, use window_context instead of page_content for the LLM
def format_with_window(retrieved_docs) -> str:
    return "

".join(
        d.metadata.get("window_context", d.page_content)
        for d in retrieved_docs
    )
Use an LLM to extract atomic

from each passage — self-contained factual claims. Each proposition becomes its own chunk. This gives the finest-grained retrieval: a query for a specific number or date will hit exactly the chunk containing that fact, not a paragraph where it is buried.

propositional chunking makes one LLM call per passage. For a 10 000-page corpus at 50 passages/page, that is 500 000 completion calls. Use it selectively for high-value, fact-dense knowledge bases where retrieval precision is worth the cost.
Fact retrieval, QA over dense text
Code or structured data (no mid-sentence facts)
Default — prevents boundary cuts for most prose

on a sample of 50–100 question-answer pairs. If context_recall is below 0.7, increase k or reduce chunk_size. If faithfulness is below 0.8, the retriever is returning irrelevant chunks — reduce chunk_size or add metadata filtering.

Splitting through the middle of a table destroys the row-column relationship. The safest approach is to treat each table as a single chunk, optionally prefixed with a header row on every chunk for context:

def chunk_table(rows: list[list[str]], source: str, page: int) -> list[dict]:
    """Serialize a table to one chunk per row (with header repeated)."""
    if not rows:
        return []
    header = " | ".join(rows[0])
    chunks = []
    for row in rows[1:]:
        row_text = " | ".join(row)
        # Repeat the header so the embedding understands column context
        chunk_text = f"[Table header: {header}]
{row_text}"
        chunks.append({"text": chunk_text, "source": source, "page": page})
    return chunks

# Alternatively: serialise the whole table as one chunk (good for small tables)
def table_to_text(rows: list[list[str]]) -> str:
    if not rows:
        return ""
    header = " | ".join(rows[0])
    divider = " | ".join(["---"] * len(rows[0]))
    body = "
".join(" | ".join(row) for row in rows[1:])
    return f"{header}
{divider}
{body}"

Code chunks must not be split at arbitrary character positions — a function with its signature on one chunk and body on another is meaningless to embed. Treat each top-level function or class as a single chunk. For files too large to fit in one chunk, split at class or function boundaries only:

import ast

def split_python_file(source_code: str, filepath: str) -> list[dict]:
    """One chunk per top-level function/class in a Python file."""
    tree = ast.parse(source_code)
    lines = source_code.splitlines(keepends=True)
    chunks = []
    for node in ast.walk(tree):
        if isinstance(node, (ast.FunctionDef, ast.AsyncFunctionDef, ast.ClassDef)):
            # Only top-level definitions
            if not isinstance(getattr(node, 'parent', None), (ast.FunctionDef, ast.ClassDef)):
                start = node.lineno - 1
                end   = node.end_lineno
                text  = "".join(lines[start:end])
                chunks.append({
                    "text": text,
                    "source": filepath,
                    "symbol": node.name,
                    "element_type": type(node).__name__,
                })
    return chunks

# For non-Python: split at blank lines between functions (heuristic)
import re
def split_code_at_functions(code: str, filepath: str) -> list[dict]:
    blocks = re.split(r"
{2,}", code.strip())
    return [{"text": b.strip(), "source": filepath} for b in blocks if b.strip()]
A chunking strategy is only as good as the retrieval quality it produces. Two RAGAS metrics directly measure chunking impact:

— fraction of ground-truth answer content that appears in retrieved chunks. Low recall (< 0.7) means relevant text is not making it into the top-K. Increase k, reduce chunk_size, or switch to a finer-grained strategy.
— fraction of retrieved chunks that were actually useful. Low precision (< 0.6) means you are retrieving too much irrelevant text. Reduce k, add metadata pre-filtering, or increase chunk_size.

# Compare two chunking strategies on 50 test questions
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import context_recall, context_precision
from datasets import Dataset

def build_eval_dataset(retriever, qa_pairs: list[dict]) -> Dataset:
    """
    qa_pairs: list of {"question": ..., "ground_truth": ...}
    Returns a Dataset suitable for ragas.evaluate().
    """
    rows = []
    for qa in qa_pairs:
        docs = retriever.invoke(qa["question"])
        rows.append({
            "question":     qa["question"],
            "ground_truth": qa["ground_truth"],
            "contexts":     [d.page_content for d in docs],
            "answer":       "",   # not needed for context metrics
        })
    return Dataset.from_list(rows)

# Strategy A: chunk_size=512, recursive
retriever_a = vectorstore_512.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 6})
# Strategy B: chunk_size=256, recursive
retriever_b = vectorstore_256.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 6})

qa_pairs = [
    {"question": "What was Q3 2025 revenue?", "ground_truth": "$2.1B"},
    # ... 49 more
]

result_a = evaluate(build_eval_dataset(retriever_a, qa_pairs),
                    metrics=[context_recall, context_precision])
result_b = evaluate(build_eval_dataset(retriever_b, qa_pairs),
                    metrics=[context_recall, context_precision])

print("512-token chunks:", result_a)
print("256-token chunks:", result_b)
# Pick the strategy with higher context_recall (up to diminishing returns).
Chunks contain two unrelated topics; embedding is ambiguous
Sentence split across boundary; key fact in neither chunk
Silent truncation; chunk tail never embedded
Use tiktoken length_function; ensure chunk_size ≤ model limit
Boilerplate lines like page numbers end up in chunks
Always normalise (deduplicate, clean whitespace) before splitting
Chunks have no source or page_number; citations impossible
split_documents() propagates metadata automatically; verify after
Using the same chunk_size for all document types
FAQs and research papers have very different density
Use a small size (128–256) for dense technical text; larger for narrative
Chunking strategy is one of the highest-leverage decisions in a RAG pipeline — invest time in measuring its impact.
RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with chunk_size=512, overlap=64, and tiktoken counting is the right default for most prose.
Semantic chunking improves recall on mixed-topic text at the cost of extra embedding calls at indexing time.
Hierarchical (ParentDocumentRetriever) gives the best of both worlds: precise child retrieval, rich parent context for the LLM.
Sentence window chunking is a lightweight alternative to hierarchical: expand ±k sentences around each matched sentence at query time.
Propositional chunking extracts atomic facts via LLM — highest precision, highest cost. Use for high-value fact-dense corpora.
Always measure context_recall with RAGAS before tuning; low recall points to retriever / chunk issues, not the LLM.
